subject: Use io.fabric8:docker-maven-plugin to build and push docker images
commit_message:
Use io.fabric8:docker-maven-plugin to build and push docker images

Addressed POLICY-1808 for drools-applications to fix the tag to be major.minor-SNAPSHOT-latest

Issue-ID: POLICY-969
Signed-off-by: Rashmi Pujar <rashmi.pujar@bell.ca>
Change-Id: I6c9ddec9a2e7a73ba68343eb98e0145ce811868e

Patch Set 6:

For #1:

I played with your patch Rashmi, the $HOME problem is maven related, it generates the wrong Dockerfile, if you look the Dockerfile under target/ it behaves as the maven resource filtering plugin is enabled.   This can be corrected 2 ways:  use $HOME to bypass filtering, or use ${HOME} but add this line to docker-controlloop/pom.xml:

diff --git a/controlloop/packages/docker-controlloop/pom.xml b/controlloop/packages/docker-controlloop/pom.xml
index e9f0ecb..bbab52b 100644
--- a/controlloop/packages/docker-controlloop/pom.xml
+++ b/controlloop/packages/docker-controlloop/pom.xml
@@ -95,6 +95,7 @@
                             <build>
                                 <cleanup>try</cleanup>
                                 <dockerFile>Dockerfile</dockerFile>
+                                <filter>false</filter>
                                 <tags>
                                     <tag>${project.version}-${maven.build.timestamp}</tag>
                                     <tag>${project.docker.latest.minmax.tag.version}</tag>

Patch Set 6:

following on my previous comment, better to disable filtering all together in pom, since there are other  ${.} that the maven plugin may expand in error before doing the docker build (#1).

For #2:

What version of docker are you using?  different versions may have a tad different behavior.   The concern is that it may fix your environment issue but break the official.  I am not familiar with the internal details on how this fabric8 plugin works, but I would assume it invokes the "docker build" underneath, so let's make sure of compatibility. 

For #3:

Right, let's switch it, since at least there would be the changes in this release related to your drools-pdp changes to use the fabric8 maven plugin.

Probably it would be a good if filtering is needed to replace all env. variables (other than the ones to be substituted by maven) to remove the ${.} to make sure that maven does not mingle with them, this will include POLICY_HOME, etc .. and should also be done in the drools-pdp Dockerfile as well, if you don't mind.

I see, not sure how that version compares with 

ubuntu@onap-orch-3:~$ docker --version
Docker version 18.09.6, build 481bc77

I think though, one difference with the previous way to build it was that the "src/main/docker/docker-entrypoint.sh" ended up after the same location under target but with executable permission .. and then the docker build will take place with the right permission.   Is it possible modifying the poms to do the equivalent as it was before?   If possible, it would be preferable ..   I see in the doc for the plugin some flags with permissions but I am not that familiar with the plugin.

Yeah, let's wait for Pam to see the reqs for the tagging also keeping besides your changes the x.y.z-label format.

Patch Set 7: Code-Review-1

To be merged after https://gerrit.onap.org/r/#/c/policy/drools-pdp/+/89203/ is merged

Patch Set 7: Code-Review-1

let's also wait till the docker entrypoint issue is resolved to prevent load breakage.

Patch Set 8:

Here is what was going on with docker-entrypoint.sh file permissions:
By default the docker-maven-plugin uses the UID root when adding any files in the docker image build. Since the base image onap/policy-drools changes the UID to "USER policy", I was unable to change the permissions on the docker-entrypoint.sh due to its default root ownership.
policy/api or policy/pap do not have this issue since they are layered on top of onap/policy-common-alpine which by default uses UID root.
In order to change the permissions on the docker-entrypoint.sh, I extracted it into fileSets definition in the assembly descriptor, and used <user> configuration in the plugin. According to the documentation file ownership changed too, but I do not see this happening. However, the file permissions are changed and the docker container starts-up just fine.
Reference: <user> element in https://dmp.fabric8.io/#build-assembly
Alternative solution:
- In drools-pdp Dockerfile set the user to root by default

I spun up locally all the containers and was able to validate that drools-pdp was able to register itself to pap
